refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the customer-defined evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is capable of performing its intended mission to total operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it explicitly defines the measurement basis (availability ratio), the target threshold (≥0.97), the evaluation period scope, and acknowledges that availability is a system-level property dependent on vehicle reliability, teleoperation robustness, safety service responsiveness, and maintenance effectiveness. High safety criticality requires that safety-driven shutdowns are counted as planned downtime, not failures.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry logging of system state transitions (operational, maintenance, safety hold, fault) across all modules; post-evaluation statistical analysis of uptime vs. total time; correlation with maintenance records and safety event logs to validate root causes of downtime.
sub_frs: FR-AVAIL-001: The Vehicle subsystem shall maintain mechanical and electrical functionality with Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) ≥ X hours.
FR-AVAIL-002: The Teleoperation subsystem shall maintain network connectivity and command/response latency within acceptable bounds with availability ≥ Y%.
FR-AVAIL-003: The Safety Services subsystem shall detect and respond to hazardous conditions within required response time with no single-point-of-failure in critical safety logic.
FR-AVAIL-004: The NGHE Solution shall implement predictive maintenance and fault diagnostics to minimize unplanned downtime.
FR-AVAIL-005: The NGHE Solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to Site Manager and Maintenance teams.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Redundant subsystem architecture with automatic failover and health monitoring across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; predictive maintenance scheduling based on component wear models and telemetry thresholds.
DP-B: High-reliability component selection (MTBF-rated parts), preventive maintenance intervals, and manual fault recovery procedures with operator training.
DP-C: Distributed fault detection and self-healing logic embedded in each subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) with centralized availability aggregation and reporting.
DP-D: Hybrid approach combining DP-A (redundancy for critical paths) and DP-C (distributed diagnostics) with DP-B (component reliability) as the foundation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid availability architecture combining (1) redundant subsystem design for critical safety and control paths (Vehicle propulsion/steering, Teleoperation command link, Safety Services hazard detection), (2) distributed fault detection and self-healing logic within each subsystem module, (3) high-reliability component selection and preventive maintenance scheduling, and (4) centralized availability aggregation and real-time status reporting to stakeholders.

potential_sources: Interdependency between Vehicle reliability and Teleoperation network availability: if teleoperation link fails, vehicle must safely transition to autonomous hold or manual recovery mode (requires coordination).
Safety Services must monitor both Vehicle and Teleoperation health to ensure safe shutdown if either subsystem degrades below acceptable thresholds (tight coupling acceptable for safety).
Maintenance scheduling across subsystems must be coordinated to avoid simultaneous downtime of redundant paths.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is well-decoupled at the logical level: each subsystem owns its own fault detection and recovery; coupling is managed through well-defined state transitions (operational → degraded → safe-hold) and explicit handoff protocols. High safety criticality justifies tight coupling in safety-critical paths.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-defined evaluation period is ≥30 days to provide statistically meaningful availability measurement.
Assumption: Redundant subsystems can be procured and integrated within project schedule and budget.
Risk: Redundancy adds cost and complexity; ROI depends on customer's downtime cost and availability target.
Risk: Distributed fault detection logic may introduce latency in system-wide availability awareness; centralized aggregation must be near-real-time.
Risk: Predictive maintenance models require historical failure data; if unavailable, fallback to fixed-interval preventive maintenance may reduce availability gains.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify with customer the relative priority of cost vs. availability target. If cost is constrained, consider phased redundancy (e.g., redundancy for Vehicle propulsion only in Phase 1, expand to Teleoperation in Phase 2). Define acceptable degraded-mode availability (e.g., single-operator manual mode) to scope redundancy requirements.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: System-level availability orchestration, including centralized health monitoring, fault aggregation, predictive maintenance scheduling, and availability reporting. Owns the integration logic that coordinates availability across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives health and status telemetry from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems; includes component MTBF data, fault events, maintenance history, and real-time operational state.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Sends maintenance scheduling tasks and fault recovery commands to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems; coordinates safe shutdown or degraded-mode transitions.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives critical safety alerts from Safety Services; sends availability-related safety status (e.g., system degradation that may affect safe operation) back to Safety Services for hazard assessment.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Receives network health and connectivity status from Teleoperation subsystem to assess teleoperation availability contribution to overall system availability.

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment: NGHE Solution is the natural system-level owner of availability orchestration, health aggregation, and reporting. All availability logic (redundancy coordination, fault detection aggregation, maintenance scheduling) naturally resides here. Minimal diffusion: only interfaces to subsystems via well-defined Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link.

Moderate containment: Vehicle owns its own propulsion/steering reliability and local fault detection. However, system-level availability orchestration (redundancy coordination, predictive maintenance scheduling, cross-subsystem health aggregation) cannot be owned by Vehicle alone; would require Vehicle to know about Teleoperation and Safety Services state, violating separation of concerns. High diffusion risk if availability logic is scattered across Vehicle and other subsystems.

Moderate-low containment: Teleoperation owns network link reliability and command/response latency. However, system-level availability (which depends on Vehicle, Safety Services, and Teleoperation together) cannot be owned by Teleoperation alone. High diffusion risk if availability aggregation logic is split between Teleoperation and other subsystems.

Low-moderate containment: Safety Services owns hazard detection and safe shutdown logic. Availability is related to safety (e.g., system must be available to respond to hazards), but Safety Services should not own system-level availability orchestration; that is a separate concern. High diffusion risk if availability logic is mixed with safety logic.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the optimal IM boundary for availability orchestration because: (1) it is the only module with visibility into all three subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), (2) it can implement centralized health aggregation and fault detection without violating subsystem autonomy, (3) it can coordinate redundancy and failover across subsystems, (4) it can schedule maintenance without creating circular dependencies, and (5) it can report availability status to stakeholders without diffusing logic across subsystems.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution becomes a bottleneck for availability decisions, latency in fault detection and recovery may increase downtime. Mitigation: implement distributed fault detection in each subsystem with NGHE Solution as aggregator and coordinator, not sole decision-maker.

Risk: NGHE Solution may lack detailed knowledge of Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services internals, leading to suboptimal availability decisions. Mitigation: define clear health status interfaces (Telemetry) that expose key availability metrics without requiring NGHE Solution to understand subsystem internals.

Risk: Predictive maintenance scheduling in NGHE Solution requires historical failure data and wear models; if data is unavailable, availability gains may not materialize. Mitigation: start with fixed-interval preventive maintenance, transition to predictive maintenance as data accumulates.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Ensure that NGHE Solution's availability orchestration logic is decoupled from subsystem-specific fault recovery. Each subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) should own its own local fault detection and recovery; NGHE Solution should own only system-level aggregation, coordination, and reporting. Define clear health status interfaces (Telemetry) to avoid tight coupling.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid availability architecture) directly addresses FR-AVAIL (availability ≥0.97) by combining redundancy, distributed fault detection, high-reliability components, and predictive maintenance. The DP is logically sound and feasible. No decoupling violations: each subsystem owns its own fault detection; NGHE Solution owns system-level orchestration.

diffusion_risks: Risk: If availability logic is scattered across NGHE Solution, Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, maintenance and evolution become difficult. Mitigation: enforce clear ownership boundaries; NGHE Solution owns orchestration, subsystems own local fault detection.

Risk: If Telemetry interface is poorly defined, NGHE Solution may not have sufficient visibility into subsystem health. Mitigation: define Telemetry schema to include key availability metrics (MTBF, fault events, maintenance status, network latency).
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the clear owner of system-level availability orchestration. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) own local fault detection and recovery. Interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimize coupling. No diffusion expected if boundaries are enforced.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via continuous telemetry logging and post-evaluation statistical analysis. NGHE Solution must implement real-time state tracking (operational, maintenance, safety-hold, fault) and expose availability metrics via Telemetry interface. Acceptance criteria (≥0.97 availability) is measurable and objective.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns system-level availability orchestration and reporting. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services own subsystem-level reliability and fault detection. Interfaces are well-defined. No ambiguity in ownership or responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Refine Telemetry interface specification to include: (1) component MTBF and failure rate data, (2) real-time fault events with timestamps, (3) maintenance schedule and completion status, (4) network latency and packet loss (for Teleoperation), (5) safety alert history. This ensures NGHE Solution has sufficient visibility for availability orchestration.

Define state transition logic in NGHE Solution: operational → degraded (one subsystem below threshold) → safe-hold (critical subsystem failed) → recovery (maintenance complete). Specify timeouts and thresholds for each transition.

Clarify with customer: is 0.97 availability measured as (total operational time) / (total time), or as (total operational time) / (total time - planned maintenance)? This affects how maintenance windows are counted.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-004 (Availability KPI) is a system-level requirement that cannot be verified at this level without decomposing into subsystem-level availability requirements. The refined FR and selected DP are sound, and the IM assignment (NGHE Solution) is correct. However, to proceed with detailed design and verification, we must decompose into sub-FRs (FR-AVAIL-001 through FR-AVAIL-005) and allocate availability targets to each subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). This requires one or more levels of functional decomposition below the current level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-AVAIL-001: The Vehicle subsystem shall maintain mechanical and electrical functionality with Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) ≥ X hours (to be determined based on system-level availability target and redundancy strategy).
FR-AVAIL-002: The Teleoperation subsystem shall maintain network connectivity and command/response latency within acceptable bounds with availability ≥ Y% (to be determined).
FR-AVAIL-003: The Safety Services subsystem shall detect and respond to hazardous conditions within required response time with no single-point-of-failure in critical safety logic.
FR-AVAIL-004: The NGHE Solution shall implement predictive maintenance and fault diagnostics to minimize unplanned downtime.
FR-AVAIL-005: The NGHE Solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to Site Manager and Maintenance teams.

assumptions: Customer-defined evaluation period is ≥30 days to provide statistically meaningful availability measurement.
Redundant subsystems can be procured and integrated within project schedule and budget.
Telemetry interface can be implemented with sufficient bandwidth and latency to support real-time availability monitoring.
Predictive maintenance models will be developed based on historical failure data (or fallback to fixed-interval preventive maintenance).
Safety-driven shutdowns are counted as planned downtime, not failures, in availability calculation.
open_risks: Risk: Redundancy adds cost and complexity; ROI depends on customer's downtime cost and availability target. Mitigation: conduct cost-benefit analysis with customer.
Risk: Distributed fault detection logic may introduce latency in system-wide availability awareness. Mitigation: define maximum latency for Telemetry updates and availability status reporting.

Risk: Predictive maintenance models require historical failure data; if unavailable, fallback to fixed-interval preventive maintenance may reduce availability gains. Mitigation: plan for data collection during initial deployment phases.

Risk: Availability target (0.97) may be difficult to achieve without significant redundancy and maintenance investment. Mitigation: validate feasibility with subsystem MTBF estimates and maintenance cost analysis.

Risk: Safety Services and Vehicle subsystems may have conflicting availability requirements (e.g., safety requires frequent testing/validation, which reduces availability). Mitigation: define acceptable degraded-mode availability and coordinate safety and availability strategies.
evidence_needed: Subsystem MTBF estimates (Vehicle propulsion, steering, electrical; Teleoperation network link; Safety Services hazard detection).
Customer's downtime cost and availability target justification (why 0.97 and not 0.95 or 0.99?).
Redundancy strategy and failover logic (which subsystems are redundant? automatic or manual failover?).
Maintenance cost and schedule (preventive maintenance intervals, predictive maintenance model, spare parts availability).
Telemetry bandwidth and latency requirements for real-time availability monitoring.
Safety-driven shutdown criteria and frequency (how often does Safety Services trigger safe-hold? what is the impact on availability?).
